item.title: Fiji Drua Women thump Brumbies 42-25
item.description: Josivini Naihamu and Repeka Tove scored doubles as the McDonald's Fiji Drua Women secured their second win of the Super Rugby Women's competition, defeating the Brumbies 42-25 at Churchill Park in Lautoka this afternoon. The Drua Women got off to a flying start, with Tove crossing over in just the second minute to give the hosts a 5-0 lead.
item.content:encoded:
Josivini Naihamu and Repeka Tove scored doubles as the McDonald's Fiji Drua Women secured their second win of the Super Rugby Women's competition, defeating the Brumbies 42-25 at Churchill Park in Lautoka this afternoon.

The Drua Women got off to a flying start, with Tove crossing over in just the second minute to give the hosts a 5-0 lead.

However, the Brumbies responded strongly and were rewarded with a try to Ashlea Bishop, who levelled the scores at 5-all.

The Drua hit back three minutes later through Naihamu to regain the lead before Sulita Waisega crossed in the 16th minute to extend the advantage to 15-5.

Bishop grabbed her second try in the 20th minute to reduce the deficit to 15-10, but the Drua continued their attacking momentum, with Merewairita Neivosa getting on the scoreboard to stretch the lead to 20-10.

With four minutes remaining in the first half, the Brumbies scored two quick tries through Bishop and Bonnie Brewer to level the scores at 20-all heading into the break.

The Drua Women regained control in the second half, running in four more tries through Naihamu, Salaseini Railumu, Karalaini Naisewa and Tove.

The Brumbies managed just one second-half try, scored by Tania Naden.

The Drua Women will now turn their attention to their clash against the Reds next Saturday.

3' Drua on attack, getting closer to the tryline. A nice cross kick from their flyhalf, and a great finish from Varanisese Qoro. Drua 5-0 BRU

5' Kolora Lomani loses the ball inside the Brumbies territory. Brumbies gets a scrum. DRU 5-0 BRU

10' Mistake from Litiana Vueti dropping the ball inches away from their tryline, giving the opportunity to Brumbies where they shifted the ball to the corner with Asheal Bishop crossing over. DRU 5-5 BRU

13' Try to Drua, Josivini Naihamu. DRU 10-5 BRU

16' Try to Drua. Great execution from Sulita Waisega. DRU 15-5 BRU

20' The Brumbies got a line-out inside the Drua's territory, they are looking for another try. DRU 15-5 BRU

22' Great play from the Brumbies, shifting the ball outwards and Asheal Bishop gets a second. DRU 15-10 BRU

27' Great play from the Drua women's team, starting with a beautiful run from Atelaite Buna, but they could not cross over the tryline as Brumbies defence stood tall. DRU 15-10 BRU

32' Penalty for Drua, they go for the line-out. DRU 15-10 BRU

35' Try to Drua. Merewairita Neivosa. DRU 20-10 BRU

38' Yellow card to Josivini Naihamu. DRU 20-15 BRU

39' Try to Brumbies. Asheal Bishop. DRU 20-20 BRU

48' Try to Drua. Salaseini Railumu. DRU 25-20 BRU

50' Brumbies gets a line-out inside the Drua's territory. They will be looking for another try. DRU 25-20 BRU

53' Great defence from the Drua girls. They held off the Brumbies attack. They get a scrum inside their own 22. DRU 25-20 BRU

61' Try to Drua. Karalaini Naisewa. DRU 30-20 BRU

68' Try to Brumbies. Tania Naden. DRU 30-25 BRU

73' Try to Drua. Repeka Tove. DRU 35-25 BRU

80' Try to Drua. Josifini Naihamu. DRU 42-25 BRU

item.title: fijivillage Straight Talk live at 7pm Wednesday on HIV cases and the work underway
item.description: A special fijivillage Straight Talk With Vijay Narayan focusing on HIV and the work of the National HIV Outbreak and Cluster Response Taskforce, will be held live at 7pm next Wednesday from Fiji Showcase 2026 at the Vodafone Arena. The guests on the show will be led by the Chair of the National HIV Outbreak and Cluster Response Taskforce, Doctor Jason Mitchell.
item.content:encoded:
A special fijivillage Straight Talk With Vijay Narayan focusing on HIV and the work of the National HIV Outbreak and Cluster Response Taskforce, will be held live at 7pm next Wednesday from Fiji Showcase 2026 at the Vodafone Arena.

The guests on the show will be led by the Chair of the National HIV Outbreak and Cluster Response Taskforce, Doctor Jason Mitchell.

We have a town-hall set up for this live show where you can also be part of the audience to participate on the show, and ask questions that you will have to send in by 8am next Tuesday.

You can send your questions focusing on the HIV statistics in Fiji, where we are at in terms of community-led health outreach, and the clinical prevention rollouts underway.

You can either send in your questions to vijaynarayan@fm96.com.fj or refer to our fijivillage social media pages to put in your questions.

If your question is selected to be asked on the show, you have to be a Fiji Showcase 2026 patron on Wednesday evening, to ask the question in person.

You can watch the show on our facebook page, YouTube channel, TikTok and X account, fijivillage at 7pm next Wednesday.

You can also catch the podcast, fijivillage Straight Talk With Vijay Narayan on Spotify and Apple Podcasts.

item.title: Land returned to Mataqali Nailagobokola of Nausori is State freehold land - Vosarogo
Skin specialists push for more space as demand grows
item.description: The Head of Dermatology at Tamavua Twomey Hospital says one of the biggest challenges facing the department is a lack of space as demand for services continues to grow. While speaking to fijivillage News, Principal Medical Officer Dermatology Dr Luciane Savo says the department is now training new dermatologists through its programme with Fiji National University, and is looking at expanding its c
item.content:encoded:
The Head of Dermatology at Tamavua Twomey Hospital says one of the biggest challenges facing the department is a lack of space as demand for services continues to grow.

While speaking to fijivillage News, Principal Medical Officer Dermatology Dr Luciane Savo says the department is now training new dermatologists through its programme with Fiji National University, and is looking at expanding its clinic space at Tamavua Twomey Hospital.

Dr Savo says they are also working to expand dermatology services to the Western and Northern Divisions so patients can access specialist care closer to home.

She says another challenge is ensuring dermatology is given greater priority within the health sector.

When asked about the department's priorities, Dr Savo says they would like to see more positions created to support services in the Central, Western and Northern Divisions.

She also highlighted the need for improved clinic facilities to enhance patient care and service delivery.

Dr Savo says the department is pushing for improvements to laboratory services, including the development of a fungal laboratory, which would help improve the diagnosis and treatment of patients.

item.title: Cancer support available, but many patients still unregistered
item.description: The Fiji Cancer Society is encouraging people to seek information, support and early assistance as cancer cases continue to rise i

item.title: Jo Nata says 2000 coup was based on lies and they were misguided fools
Residents raise concerns over prolonged power outage in Naduru, Nausori
item.description: Residents of Prakash Feeder Road in Naduru, Nausori are calling on Energy Fiji Limited to urgently restore power after an extended outage disrupted the lives of around 40 households. One resident told fijivillage News that power was switched off at around 6pm on Thursday and was restored at about 1pm yesterday before being cut off again at around 6pm.
item.content:encoded:
Residents of Prakash Feeder Road in Naduru, Nausori are calling on Energy Fiji Limited to urgently restore power after an extended outage disrupted the lives of around 40 households.

One resident told fijivillage News that power was switched off at around 6pm on Thursday and was restored at about 1pm yesterday before being cut off again at around 6pm.

She says she contacted EFL's toll-free number and was informed that the outage was linked to the fuel crisis and that repair crews do not work after 5pm or on weekends.

She claims they were told power may not be restored until Monday.

She adds that many families in the area are working parents who rely on weekends to complete household chores.

HINDI INSERT: Resident raises concerns over power cut June 20

The resident is calling on EFL to maintain emergency response teams to deal with such situations and restore power as quickly as possible.

Meanwhile, in response to our questions, EFL has clarified that the outage is most likely due to a fault and not the result of the fuel crisis.

The company says restoration work on faults is carried out on the next working day.

item.title: $5M allocated to support sugarcane farmers with rising fuel costs
item.description: The Ministry of Sugar Industry has revealed that a total of $5 million has been allocated from its 2025/2026 budget to support sugarcane farmers in meeting the rising fuel costs of cartage and mechanical harvesting. The Ministry says the funding has been reallocated from its existing programmes within the sugar industry and agriculture sector.
item.content:encoded:
The Ministry of Sugar Industry has revealed that a total of $5 million has been allocated from its 2025/2026 budget to support sugarcane farmers in meeting the rising fuel costs of cartage and mechanical harvesting.

The Ministry says the funding has been reallocated from its existing programmes within the sugar industry and agriculture sector.

Minister for Sugar Tomasi Tunabuna confirms that the $5 million will be used to subsidise diesel fuel at $2 per litre and will only fund lorries, mechanical harvesters, locomotives and tractors used in cane harvesting and cartage activities.

The Ministry says the fuel subsidy will cover the period from today to August 31st this year as temporary relief.

They say all lorries, tractors used for cane cartage, mechanical harvesters and locomotives must be registered with the Fiji Sugar Corporation and comply with Land Transport Authority requirements.

The Ministry says the $2 per litre diesel fuel subsidy will go directly to sugarcane farmers. However, the mechanism for distributing the subsidy will be decided collectively by FSC, the Sugar Industry Tribunal and the Sugar Cane Growers Council.

They say measures will be put in place to ensure there is no double-dipping by beneficiaries, while the utilisation of the subsidy will be strictly monitored by the Sugar Cane Growers Council and FSC to prevent misuse.

The Ministry says the subsidy will be provided on a first come, first served basis and will take into account the volume of cane harvested and delivered to the mills.

They say the programme will end on August 31st or when the subsidy has been fully utilised, whichever comes first.

They add that the subsidy will be managed by FSC and released through the existing payment system following verification.

According to industry records, approximately 4.6 million litres of diesel fuel were used by lorries, locomotives, mechanical harvesters and tractors for harvesting and transporting cane during the 2025 season. This excludes about 1.2 million litres used by Fiji Sugar Corporation diesel generators at sugar factories during cane crushing.

The Ministry says that, of the 4.6 million litres used for harvesting and cartage, about 2.3 million litres were consumed during the first three months of the season, from June to August.

Farmers are being encouraged to consult FSC, the Sugar Cane Growers Council and the Sugar Industry Tribunal for further clarification on the implementation and disbursement of the subsidy.

Meanwhile, Tunabuna is urgently calling on sugarcane farmers, lorry operators, mechanical harvester and tractor operators, as well as sugar factories, to commence sugarcane harvesting and cane crushing.

The Ministry says it remains committed to the sugarcane sector and will ensure the long-term sustainability of the industry.

item.description: The new lawyer for Imran Khan, also known as 'King Khan', has now filed an application in the High Court to vacate Khan's guilty p
